**Agree.**

The police forced entry **because of a perceived emergency involving Joanne** (based on her frantic call reporting loud crashing noises in her home), and the resulting damage fits the policy’s definition of “Emergency Damages,” even though it later turned out there was no actual emergency.
Agree.

**Agree.**

The policy covers damage caused when first responders force entry **because of an emergency or a perceived emergency** involving the policyholder. Here, Joanne reported what she believed to be a burglary, and the police forced entry to respond to that **perceived emergency**, even though it later turned out to be paranoia.

**Disagree.** The policy covers damage caused when **first responders** force entry due to an emergency involving Salma. Here, the forced entry was made by **Sam (an accountant and Salma’s friend), not a first responder**, so the damage does not fall within the defined “Emergency Damages” coverage.

Disagree.

The policy covers **loss or damage to the contents** of the home caused by first responders forcing entry. Damage from a forced entry is typically to the **structure/door/lock**, which is not “contents,” so it would fall outside this “Emergency Damages” definition (even though the police entry was due to a real emergency involving Salma).

Disagree.

Although there was an emergency involving Salma (a burglary), the policy only covers damage caused when **“first responders”** force entry. Sam is an **off-duty firefighter acting as a private individual/friend**, not responding in an official first-responder capacity. That makes the forced entry unlikely to fall within the policy’s “Emergency Damages” definition. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

Disagree.

The policy covers “Escape of Oil” only when it involves oil leaking from a **fixed oil‑fired heating installation**. Madison’s loss came from **spilling kerosene she was carrying**, not a leak from a fixed heating system, so it doesn’t fall within the defined coverage.
**Agree.** The damage is covered because Madison’s loss resulted from **oil leaking from a fixed oil-fired heating installation**, which matches the policy’s definition of “Escape of Oil.”
**Agree.** The damage resulted from oil escaping (leaking/rushing) from a **fixed oil‑fired heating installation**, which matches the policy’s definition of “Escape of Oil.”

Disagree.

The policy covers “Escape of Oil” only when the oil leaks from a **fixed oil‑fired heating installation**. Mariam’s loss came from a **portable oil lamp**, which is not a fixed heating installation, so it falls outside the definition of covered “Escape of Oil.”

**Agree.** Mariam’s damage was caused by oil leaking from a **fixed oil-fired heating installation** (the cracked heater), which fits the policy’s definition of “Escape of Oil,” so the claim should be covered.

Disagree.

The policy covers “Escape of Oil” only when the oil leaks from a **fixed oil-fired heating installation**. A wall-mounted oil lamp is not an oil-fired heating installation, so the damage would not fall within that defined coverage.
Disagree.

Agree.

The wall damage was caused by a **sudden, unforeseen flow of water from an internal source** (a **pipe that suddenly burst**), which matches the policy’s definition of “Escape of Water” damage.
Agree.

**Disagree.**

Even though the leak was unexpected and occurred inside the home, “Escape of Water” coverage is typically aimed at water escaping from the home’s internal plumbing or connected domestic systems (e.g., burst pipes, faulty appliances). A leaking goldfish bowl is not an internal water installation or appliance of that kind, so it would not fall within the defined “Escape of Water” peril.
Agree. Final answer is: **Agree**
Agree

Disagree.

The policy covers damage caused by a sudden, unforeseen flow of **water** from an internal source. Here, the tub leaked **paint**, not water, so the loss does not fit the defined peril of “Escape of Water,” even if the crack and leak were sudden and unforeseen.

**Agree.**  
The damage was caused by a **sudden, unforeseen escape/flow of water from an internal source** (the bathtub/fixture within the home), which fits the policy’s definition of “Escape of Water” damage.

Disagree.

The policy covers “Escape of Water” damage—i.e., a sudden, unforeseen flow of **water** from an internal source. Here, the escaping liquid is **tomato juice**, not water, so it does not fall within the defined covered peril even though the leak was sudden and came from inside the home.

Disagree.

The policy defines covered “Fire” damage as damage caused by a **naturally-occurring fire**. Here, the garage fire was triggered by a **defective gas grill/propane tank explosion** (a human-made, non-natural source), so it does not fit the policy’s definition of covered fire damage.

**Agree.** The garage damage was caused by the **brush fire**, which is described as a naturally occurring fire that reached her property before the grill explosion, so it falls within the policy’s definition of covered “Fire” damage.

**Agree.** The damage to Fatema’s garage was set in motion by a **naturally-occurring brush fire** (a covered “Fire” peril), whose embers caused the propane explosion and resulting garage fire. Under typical proximate/efficient-cause analysis, that chain of events keeps the loss within the policy’s “Fire” coverage.

Disagree.

The policy defines “Flooding” as a large volume of water caused by a sudden release **from outside the buildings**. Here, the water came from a leaking waterbed **inside** the home, so it does not meet the policy’s definition of Flooding. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

**Agree.** The damage fits the policy definition of “Flooding” because a **large volume of water** **suddenly released from outside the building** (the fire hydrant) **invaded the property** and caused damage.

**Agree.** The water came from **outside the building** (the rain barrel), involved a **large volume of water**, and resulted from a **sudden release** that invaded the property, fitting the policy’s definition of “Flooding.”

Disagree.

The policy defines “Flooding” as water invasion caused by a **rapid build-up** from outside the building. Here, the creek level **rose gradually over several years**, so the damage does not fit the “rapid build-up” requirement in the definition.
**Agree.** Final answer is: **Agree**

Disagree.

The policy defines “Flooding” as an invasion caused by a **rapid build-up** of water from outside. Here, the creek rose **gradually over about a week**, so it likely does not meet the policy’s “rapid” requirement. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers damage to plants **caused by** civil commotion/strikes/labor or political disturbances. Here, the strike only made it inconvenient for Genie to buy fertilizer; it did not directly damage the plants. The plants died from lack of fertilizer, which is an indirect, non-covered cause under this definition.

**Agree.** The plants were within Genie’s property boundaries, and the damage occurred as a direct result of **strike-related activity** (picketing spilling onto her land). That fits the policy’s definition of covered “Garden Plants” damage caused by **strikes**.

Disagree.

The Garden Plants coverage applies to **direct damage to plants caused by** events like **civil commotion or strikes** (e.g., plants being destroyed during strike-related unrest). Here, the strike only caused a **fertilizer shortage**, and the plants died from **lack of fertilizer/maintenance**, not from direct strike-related physical damage. This is an indirect, consequential loss and would not fall within the defined covered causes.
Disagree.

**Agree.** The damage was to garden plants within her property boundaries and was caused by masked individuals acting maliciously/vandalizing, which fits the policy definition of covered “Garden Plants” damage.

Disagree.

The Garden Plants coverage applies only to damage caused by **malicious people or vandals**. The facts given show the neighbor’s children **trampled** the plants, but don’t establish that the damage was **willful/malicious (vandalism)** rather than accidental or careless. Without that “malicious/vandal” element, the loss wouldn’t clearly fall within the defined covered cause.

Final answer is: **Disagree**

**Disagree.**

Gavin’s injury happened while he was off work doing a personal task (putting up holiday lights) and not “under regular working conditions.” Therefore, his lost income from missing work would not fall under the policy’s “General Damages” coverage as described.

**Agree.** Gavin’s injury happened while performing his normal job duties under regular working conditions (accessing a second-story A/C unit via a ladder), and the resulting missed work/lost income fits the policy’s “General Damages” coverage description.

**Disagree.**

Gavin’s injury happened while doing a personal favor at his girlfriend’s home, not **under his regular working conditions** (i.e., while performing his job in the course of employment). Even though the activity was similar to his work, it wasn’t work-related under the policy language, so the lost-income claim wouldn’t be covered under “General Damages” as described.
Disagree.

**Agree.** Shannon was performing “Hot Work” because she was using a welding tool (a heat/flame source capable of igniting flammable materials) while working on her motorcycle (her belongings), and the resulting fire caused accidental damage to her home/garage and belongings—so it falls within the policy’s definition. Final answer is: **Agree**.

**Agree.**

Shannon was doing work on her **belonging** (the motorcycle) using equipment (a gasoline-engine air compressor) that **produces heat** and could **ignite flammable materials**. Since the fire resulted from that heat source overheating and igniting a nearby flammable product, the damage falls within the policy’s definition of accidental damage from **“Hot Work.”**

Agree.

Using a heat gun to dry flooring glue is “Hot Work” under the policy’s broad definition (it uses/produces heat that could ignite flammable materials), and the table damage was accidental and caused by that hot work.

**Agree.**  

Martha’s use of a heat gun to dry flooring glue is “Hot Work” because it **uses a source of heat that could ignite flammable materials**, and the fire damage was accidental and arose directly from that hot work activity.

**Agree.** Using a heat gun to dry glue is “Hot Work” because it uses a heat source that could ignite flammable materials, and the table burn was accidental damage resulting from that Hot Work. Final answer is: **Agree**.

Disagree.

The policy covers damage caused **while being removed by professional removal contractors**. Denise moved her belongings herself (she rented a truck and did not use professional movers), so the loss does not fall within the “House Removal” definition.

Disagree.

The policy covers damage **caused while belongings are being removed from the home by professional removal contractors**. Here, the damage happened **during transit in a truck accident**, after the items had already been removed from the home, so it doesn’t fall within the stated definition of “House Removal” coverage.

Disagree.

The “House Removal” coverage applies only when the belongings are being removed by **professional removal contractors**. Denise’s uncle, although formerly a professional mover, is helping her informally and is not acting as a contracted professional removal company for this move, so the loss would not fall within the defined coverage.

Disagree.

Harper’s policy covers damage to belongings **while being stored by professional removal contractors**. Bill is her cousin, not a professional removal contractor, so the flooding damage while stored at his house would not fall within the “House Removal” coverage as described.

**Agree.** Harper’s belongings were being **stored by professional removal contractors** (the moving company) as part of her move, and the damage occurred during that storage, so it falls within the policy’s “House Removal” coverage as described.
Disagree.

**Disagree.**

Although Jason’s losses are from “identity theft,” the theft arose out of the hacking of his **car dealership’s computer network**, meaning it is **connected with his business**. Because the policy excludes identity theft connected with the policyholder’s business, the claim would likely be **excluded from coverage**.

Agree.  

The identity theft (hackers stealing Jason’s SSN from his home WiFi and opening fraudulent personal credit lines) is not “connected with [his] business” as a car salesman, so the business-connection exclusion shouldn’t apply. Therefore, Jason’s losses (including legal fees to resolve the fraud, if covered under the policy’s loss definitions) should be covered.

Disagree.

Because the identity theft was enabled through Jason’s **work laptop** (a business-related device), the loss is plausibly “connected with the policyholder’s business,” which falls within the policy’s stated exclusion.
Disagree.

**Agree.**

The identity theft occurred through hacking Jack’s **home** WiFi and involved theft of his **credit card information** for fraudulent purchases. Nothing in the facts indicates the theft was **connected with his occupation** as a business consultant, so the occupational-connection exclusion should not apply. Therefore, Jack should be covered under the Identity Theft policy.

**Disagree.**

Although Jack suffered “identity theft,” the loss is **connected with his occupation** because the stolen information came from a restaurant network **used for his work-related meetings**. That connection triggers the policy’s exclusion for “identity theft connected with the policyholder’s occupation,” so the claim would likely **not be covered**.

Disagree.

The policy only covers loss or accidental damage **while Vivian is temporarily away from home**. Because she **permanently moved abroad**, she was not “temporarily away,” so the fire damage to the house would not fall within that coverage provision.

Disagree.

The “Loss and Accidental Damage” coverage you described applies only when the **property is temporarily away from the home**. Vivian’s **house itself** was damaged **at the home location**, so this specific “away from home” accidental damage coverage would not apply to the fire damage.

Disagree.

Because Vivian **moved abroad** (even if she hopes to return someday), she is not “**temporarily away from home**” in the ordinary insurance/contract sense. Therefore the “Loss and Accidental Damage” coverage that applies only while she is **temporarily away** would not cover the fire damage to her house.
**Disagree.** The policy covers key replacement **only in the case of theft**, and Peter **lost** the key (no theft), so this loss is not covered under that provision.

**Agree.** The van is a goods-carrying vehicle used for deliveries, and the key was taken in a mugging (a form of theft), so the policy’s “key replacement in the case of theft” provision should apply.

**Disagree.**

The policy only includes **key replacement “in the case of theft.”** Here, Peter **voluntarily gave the van (and key) to an acquaintance**, and the key was **not returned**—that’s not clearly a **theft** event as required by the coverage wording. Without proof that the key was **stolen** (i.e., taken with intent to permanently deprive), the key replacement cost would typically **not be covered** under that provision.

**Final answer: Disagree.**

Disagree.

The policy covers **deliberate** destruction or damage (“malicious acts or vandalism”). The garbage truck incident was described as **accidental**, not intentional, so it doesn’t fit that coverage category. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

Agree

The teenagers deliberately interfered with Olivia’s property by dumping garbage on it, causing damage in the form of contamination/litter and resulting cleanup costs. That fits the policy’s definition of “actions involving deliberate destruction or damage to the policyholder’s property.”

Disagree

The teenagers’ conduct was reckless and caused damage, but it doesn’t fit the policy’s definition of “Malicious Acts or Vandalism,” which requires *deliberate* destruction or damage to Olivia’s property.
Disagree.
Agree.

Agree

The graffiti was deliberate damage, and it was caused by someone other than Beth (an adverse third party). On these facts, the loss fits the policy’s definition of “Malicious Acts or Vandalism,” so the repainting/repair costs should be covered.
**Disagree.** Gwen’s injury occurred while loading groceries into the car, not **“while traveling in your car,”** so it likely falls outside the policy’s Personal Accident definition.
**Agree.** Gwen’s wrist injury was accidental and occurred while she was traveling in (driving) her car, so it fits the policy’s definition of “Personal Accident.”
Disagree.

**Disagree.**

Although Penelope’s injury was accidental and occurred while she was traveling in her car, the policy only covers injuries that result in **permanent and total loss of use of one or more limbs**. Because her knee injury was **temporary** and she **fully recovered**, it does not meet the policy’s definition of “Personal Accident,” so she is **not covered** under that provision.

**Disagree.**

Although Penelope’s injury occurred while she was traveling in her car (so the “accidental injury while traveling” part is satisfied), the policy only pays if the accident results in **“permanent and total loss of use of one or more limbs.”** Losing function in her **left knee** is not clearly the same as a **total loss of use of an entire limb** (e.g., the whole leg), which is what this kind of language typically requires.

**Disagree.**

Although Penelope’s injury occurred while she was traveling in her car, the policy requires that the accident **result in “permanent and total loss of use”** of a limb. Here, the doctor’s prognosis is that she **would regain full function** with three months of physical therapy, so the injury is **not medically “permanent”** in the sense required by the definition. Her later non-recovery is due to not undertaking treatment, not because the accident itself necessarily caused a permanent loss.

**Disagree.**

Although the damage is to a third party’s property (which would normally fall under Public Liability Property Damages), Dave **failed to take reasonable steps to prevent the damage** because he knowingly drove with a long-standing brake warning light and unresponsive brakes. That triggers the policy exclusion, so the insurer can deny coverage.

**Agree.** Dave is legally liable for damage to third-party property, and he appears to have taken reasonable steps to prevent the damage (regularly checking the manual and getting scheduled maintenance), so the exclusion likely doesn’t apply.

**Disagree.**

Even though the damage is to **third‑party property** and Dave is **legally liable** (so it fits the basic definition of “Public Liability Property Damages”), the policy excludes coverage if the insured **failed to take reasonable steps to prevent the damage**. Because Dave **knew** the car was **overdue for scheduled maintenance** and still drove it, the insurer can argue he did not take reasonable steps to prevent a brake-related accident, so the exclusion applies.

**Final answer: Disagree.**
Disagree. The roof damage was caused by Tom dropping the toolbox due to the phone ringing, not by the wind itself, so it doesn’t fit the policy’s definition of “Wind Damage.”
Agree. Final answer is: **Agree**

Agree.

The damage resulted from winds with gusts over 55 mph, and the waves were a direct effect of those winds, so the loss is “damage from wind” as defined.

Disagree.

Even though the storm caused the power outage, the roof damage was caused by the fire that started when Cam dropped the candle—an intervening, independent cause. Under the usual “proximate cause” approach in insurance, that makes the loss not “damage caused by a storm” as defined by the policy.
Agree.

**Agree.** The lightning storm set in motion the chain of events (lightning strike → power surge → heater fire → roof damage), so the roof damage was **caused by a storm** under the policy’s definition.

Disagree.

The policy defines “Hail Damage” as hail **of such intensity that it causes damage to hard surfaces or breaks glass**. Here, the only described damage is to **plants** on the balcony, with no indication that the hail damaged hard surfaces or broke glass, so the loss doesn’t fall within the policy’s defined coverage.
Agree.
Agree.

Disagree.

The damage was caused by vibrations from people dancing inside the house, not by “ground heave of the site on which the building stands” or a “landslip,” so it doesn’t fit the policy’s Ground Heave definition.

**Disagree.** The damage described is from **earthquake shaking**, not from **ground heave (upward ground movement) or landslip** as defined in the policy, so it would not be covered under the Ground Heave provision.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers damage **resulting from “ground heave of the site on which the building stands, or landslip”**—i.e., movement of the ground/soil itself (uplift/expansion or slipping). Damage caused by **vibrations/shock from a nearby truck collision** is not “ground heave” or “landslip,” so it would not fall within that coverage definition.

**Disagree.** The policy covers damage resulting from **ground heave or landslip** (movement of the ground beneath the building). Clint’s roof collapse was due to **water damage**, not ground movement, so it would not fall under the Ground Heave coverage.

Agree.

The damage resulted from the ground beneath the house shifting (ground heave of the site on which the building stands), which fits the policy definition of covered “Ground Heave” damage. Final answer is: **Agree**.

Disagree.

A tree falling onto the roof is not “loss or damage resulting from ground heave … or landslip” of the site the building stands on, so it wouldn’t be covered under the Ground Heave provision.

Disagree.

The “Trace and Access” coverage applies to necessary and reasonable costs to **locate and fix accidental damage to cables or pipes**. Here, Justin is tearing out drywall and floorboards to **retrieve a dropped ring**, not to locate or repair **accidental damage to a pipe or cable**. Since the access work isn’t tied to fixing damage to the plumbing system itself, it wouldn’t fall within the defined coverage.

Disagree.

“Trace and Access” applies to the **necessary and reasonable costs** of locating and accessing **accidental** damage to pipes/cables. Here, the scenario describes a **leak and more extensive pipe damage discovered during repairs**, which is more consistent with **gradual deterioration/wear and tear** rather than an “accidental” event, so the Trace and Access coverage would not apply.

Final answer is: **Disagree**

Disagree.

“Trace and Access” generally covers the necessary and reasonable cost to *locate and access* hidden pipes/cables to fix **accidental damage or a leak event**, not damage arising from the insured’s own repair/maintenance work (i.e., DIY/workmanship-related damage). Since Justin punctured the pipe while doing a repair, that’s typically treated as damage caused by his own work rather than an insured “trace and access” situation.

**Disagree.**

The Trace and Access cover applies only to the **necessary and reasonable costs of locating and fixing _accidental damage_** to pipes/cables/drains/tanks. Here, the low water pressure is caused by **faulty/poor installation (workmanship)**, not accidental damage to the piping. Because the underlying problem is not “accidental damage,” the Trace and Access provision would not respond to pay for the access/removal costs (e.g., lifting floorboards) or related repairs.

**Agree.** The cracked water pipes are **accidental damage to pipes providing services to the home**, and the **costs to locate and access the damage** (including removing floorboards) fall within the “Trace and Access” definition.

**Disagree.**

“Trace and Access” typically covers the **costs of locating and accessing** a **sudden, accidental** failure (e.g., a burst pipe). Here, the pipes **cracked over time due to long-term wear and tear**, which is **not accidental damage**, so the trace-and-access cover would not apply.

**Disagree.**

Because Jenny left the car in her friend’s custody/control with permission, and the friend is **not covered under the policy**, the policy’s exclusion applies **even though the damage was caused by a falling utility pole**. Therefore, the Vehicle Damage coverage would not cover this loss.

**Agree.** The exclusion applies only if, at the time of the incident, the car was in the custody or control of a non-covered person who had Jenny’s permission. Here, the car was simply parked on the street and damaged by a falling utility pole, with no such person in custody or control of it.

**Agree.**

The exclusion applies only if, **at the time of the incident**, the car was **under the custody or control** of someone (with Jenny’s permission) who isn’t covered. Here, the car was simply **parked on the street** and was damaged by a falling utility pole—there’s no indication the neighbor had actual **custody/control of the vehicle itself** at that moment (having a key for safekeeping isn’t the same as having custody/control of the car). Therefore, the Vehicle Damage coverage should apply.

Disagree.

The rust developed gradually over the years, which is typical “wear and tear,” and the policy expressly excludes loss or damage caused by wear and tear. Therefore, the wheel replacement would not be covered under the Vehicle Damage policy.
Agree.

**Agree.**

The damage (both the misalignment and the resulting scraping) traces back to the **accident**, not ordinary **wear and tear**. The “wear and tear” exclusion typically applies to gradual deterioration from normal use, not damage that occurs because an accident knocked the wheel out of alignment and then caused further harm.

**Disagree.** The “Vehicle Damage” coverage applies only to damage to Steve’s **car** or **car accessories** (while in or on the car). A **guitar** is personal property, not a car accessory, so it isn’t covered under this policy provision.
Agree.

Disagree.

A tablet computer—even if used for navigation—is generally considered personal property, not a “car accessory” (which typically means equipment that is part of the vehicle or designed/installed for use with it). Since the policy covers the car itself and “car accessories (while in or on the car),” the tablet likely falls outside that definition.

Disagree.

The “Vehicle Damage” coverage applies to (1) Chris’s car or (2) audio equipment while in the car. A work laptop is neither, so it isn’t covered under that policy definition.

Agree.

The “Vehicle Damage” coverage includes loss or damage to “audio equipment (while in the car),” and Chris’s car stereo system and speakers are audio equipment in the car, so the damage is covered.

Agree.

Chris’s portable Bluetooth speaker qualifies as “audio equipment,” and it was “while in the car” at the time of the accident, so it falls within the policy’s definition of covered “Vehicle Damage.”

Disagree.

The policy covers **“Vehicle Damage,” defined as “damage”** to the car or to car accessories (including child car seats while in or on the car). Here, the car seat was **stolen (a loss/theft), not damaged**, so it doesn’t fall within the policy’s defined “Vehicle Damage” coverage.

**Agree.** The policy covers damage to “car accessories, including children’s car seats (while in or on the car).” At the time of the crash, the car seat was **on** the car (on the trunk), so it falls within the definition of covered “Vehicle Damage.”

**Agree.**

The policy covers damage to “car accessories, including children’s car seats (while in or on the car).” At the time of the accident, the car seat was **on** the car (on the trunk), so the damage falls within the defined “Vehicle Damage” coverage.

Disagree.

The “Vehicle Theft” coverage applies to theft of **the car itself** or **audio equipment in/on the car**. Gisele’s claim is for a stolen **briefcase and work laptop**, which are personal property and not included in that definition (and no covered theft/damage to the car or audio equipment is described).

**Agree.** The policy covers loss or damage due to theft of the policyholder’s **audio equipment while in or on the car**, and the stereo was stolen from Gisele’s car (as shown by the surveillance footage). Final answer is: **Agree**.

**Agree.** The stolen item (a wireless Bluetooth headset) is reasonably considered **audio equipment**, and it was stolen **while in the car** as a result of a theft, which fits the policy’s “Vehicle Theft” definition.

**Disagree.**

The “Vehicle Fire” coverage applies to **loss or damage from fire to Gene’s car or its audio equipment (while in/on the car)**. Gene’s claim is for **destroyed bills (personal property)**, which are **not the car or audio equipment**, so that loss is not covered under the stated definition.

**Agree.** The policy covers “loss or damage as a result of fire” to the car or its audio equipment while in/on the car. Even though the fire started in the smartphone, it spread and caused fire damage to the stereo speaker (audio equipment in the car), so the loss falls within the Vehicle Fire coverage. Final answer is: **Agree**.

**Agree.**

The policy covers loss or damage **resulting from fire** to **(1) the car** or **(2) audio equipment while in or on the car**. Here, a fire occurred and damaged at least the **portable Bluetooth speaker (audio equipment)** (and potentially the car), so that fire damage falls within the “Vehicle Fire” coverage definition—even though the fire started with a defective smartphone (which itself is not covered).

**Agree.** The smoke damage was caused by a **smoldering (burning) cigarette**, which is a form of **fire/combustion**, so the loss is “as a result of fire” under the Vehicle Fire coverage—even though nothing else in the car ignited into open flames.
**Agree.** Ken’s car was destroyed **as a result of fire**, which falls squarely within the policy’s “Vehicle Fire” coverage definition, regardless of where the fire originated.

**Agree.** The car suffered damage (smoke damage to the interior) **as a result of a fire**, and the policy’s “Vehicle Fire” definition does not restrict the fire to originating in the car itself or require flames to spread to the vehicle.

Disagree.

The “Vehicle Theft” coverage applies to theft-related loss or damage **to the car itself or to car accessories** (while in or on the car). Hunter’s claim is for **stolen cash**, which is neither the vehicle nor a car accessory, so that loss is not covered under this provision (though any physical damage to the car from the break-in might be covered separately if the policy covers that).
Agree. The seat covers are car accessories located in/on the car, and they were stolen, so the loss falls within the policy’s “Vehicle Theft” coverage as defined. Final answer is: **Agree**.

Disagree.

The “Vehicle Theft” coverage applies to theft of the car itself or “car accessories” while in or on the car. A smartphone is generally considered personal property, not a car accessory (unless it’s a permanently installed vehicle component), so its theft would not be covered under this provision.

Disagree.

Because the car was taken by Dillon’s personal assistant (an employee), the “Vehicle Theft” coverage exclusion for theft by an employee applies, so the loss is not covered.

**Agree.** The theft was committed by an unfamiliar person, not a family/household member or an (ex-)employee, so the policy’s exclusion doesn’t apply and Dillon’s loss is covered under the Vehicle Theft coverage.

**Disagree.**

Even though Dillon and his wife have been separated for over two years, she is still his **spouse** (i.e., a **member of his family**) unless they are legally divorced. The policy excludes theft “if your car was taken by a member of your **family or household**,” so the theft by his estranged wife falls within the exclusion and is not covered.

**Disagree.** The Vehicle Theft coverage applies only if Cooper’s car contains a working GPS tracking device, and he did not have one in the car, so the theft would not be covered under that provision.
Agree.

Agree.

Cooper’s car “contains a working GPS tracking device” because the smartphone in the car has GPS tracking capability and is working at the time of the theft, so the Vehicle Theft coverage condition is satisfied. Final answer is: **Agree**.
Disagree.
Agree.
Disagree.

**Disagree.** The “Vehicle Glass” coverage applies only to **broken glass** and **scratching to the bodywork caused solely and directly by broken glass**. The damage described is to the **front hood from a baseball impact**, not from broken glass, so it is not covered under this provision.

**Agree.** The windshield damage is “broken glass,” and the scratches to the bodywork were caused solely and directly by the broken glass shards, so both fall within the Vehicle Glass coverage definition.

**Agree.** The side mirror’s glass is **broken glass**, and the scratches to the bodywork were caused **solely and directly by the broken glass**, which matches the policy’s “Vehicle Glass” definition.

**Disagree.**

Even though there was no actual emergency, the police forced entry **because of a perceived emergency involving Joanne** (based on her frantic call). The policy definition includes both an **emergency or perceived emergency**, so the forced-entry damage fits the covered “Emergency Damages” scenario.

Disagree.
